name: Linear Inequalities — Ranges, Not Just One Answer
whatIsIt: An inequality uses <, >, ≤ or ≥ instead of =, so the answer is a RANGE of values, not a single number. You solve it like an equation — do the same thing to both sides — with ONE golden rule: if you multiply or divide both sides by a NEGATIVE number, you must FLIP the inequality sign. Solutions are shown on a number line (an open circle ○ for < or >, a filled circle ● for ≤ or ≥).
whyNeeded: Real limits are ranges: 'spend at most ₹500', 'score at least 40 to pass', 'the lift holds up to 8 people'. Inequalities model budgets, safety limits, and optimisation — the maths of 'no more than' and 'at least'.
realLifeUses: A budget: total cost must be at most ₹500
A lift's limit: no more than 8 people
Age limits: must be 18 or older

vocabulary.meaning: A statement with <, >, ≤, or ≥ instead of =, giving a range of answers.
Reverse < to > (and ≤ to ≥) when multiplying or dividing by a negative number.
All the values that make the inequality true.

story.title: The Ticket Budget
story.text: Sara has ₹200 and each ride costs ₹40. 'How many rides can I take?' she asks. Bhai writes 40x ≤ 200, meaning the cost must be at most 200. Divide both sides by 40: x ≤ 5. 'You can take up to 5 rides,' he smiles. 'Not exactly 5 — 5 OR FEWER. That's the difference an inequality makes.'
story.extractedProblem: Each ride is ₹40 and Sara has ₹200. Solve 40x ≤ 200 for x.
story.answerInStory: x ≤ 5 — she can take at most 5 rides (200 ÷ 40 = 5).

lines.label: x ≤ 5 — filled circle at 5, shaded to the left

visual.caption: The solution x ≤ 5 is a whole range on the number line: everything up to and including 5 (a filled circle ● at 5).

teachingGallery.title: Solve, then flip if negative
teachingGallery.note: Same steps as an equation; flip the sign only when you multiply or divide by a negative.

examples.caption: x > 4: open circle ○ (4 not included), shaded to the right.
Budget inequality: repeated cost must stay under the total.

standardMethod.summary: Isolate the variable using inverse operations, exactly like an equation. If you multiply or divide both sides by a negative, FLIP the sign. Show the range on a number line.
steps: Get the variable term alone using +, −, ×, ÷ on both sides.
If you multiply or divide by a NEGATIVE number, FLIP the inequality sign.
Write the solution as a range (e.g. x ≤ 5).
Draw it: open circle ○ for < / >, filled circle ● for ≤ / ≥, then shade the correct side.
Check with one value from the range.
Divide both sides by 2 (positive → no flip).
Divide both sides by −2 — NEGATIVE, so flip > to <.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not flipping the inequality when dividing or multiplying by a negative (e.g. −2x > 6 giving x > −3).
commonMistakes.fix: Multiplying or dividing by a NEGATIVE flips the sign: −2x > 6 becomes x < −3.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using a filled circle for < or > (or an open circle for ≤ / ≥).
commonMistakes.fix: ○ open = NOT included (< or >). ● filled = included (≤ or ≥).

easy.q: Solve 2x < 10. The largest WHOLE number x can be is?
Solve x + 3 > 7. The smallest WHOLE number x can be is?

medium.q: Solve 3x ≤ 12. The largest value x can be is?
Solve −2x > 6. The largest WHOLE number x can be is?

challenge.q: Solving −x < 2, Ravi writes x < −2. What's the fix?

questions.q: Solve x − 2 ≥ 3. Smallest value x can be?
Solve −3x ≥ 9. Largest value x can be?
For x ≤ 5 on a number line you draw…

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up a budget like 40x ≤ 200 and solve it to x ≤ 5, explaining it means 'at most 5'. Then show −2x > 6 and explain why dividing by −2 FLIPS the sign to x < −3.

revisionCard.summary: Inequalities (<, >, ≤, ≥) give a RANGE. Solve like an equation, but FLIP the sign when multiplying/dividing by a negative. Number line: ○ open for < / > (not included), ● filled for ≤ / ≥ (included). Shade the side that makes it true.

rememberIt.hook: Solve like an equation - but FLIP the sign when you x or / by a negative
rememberIt.unpack: -2x < 6 -> divide by -2 AND flip -> x > -3. The answer is a RANGE, not one number.

studentQuestions.q: When do I flip the inequality sign?
studentQuestions.a: Only when you multiply or divide BOTH sides by a NEGATIVE number. Then < becomes > and vice versa.

studentQuestions.q: How is it different from an equation?
studentQuestions.a: Same steps to isolate x, but the answer is a range (like x > 3), not a single value - and watch the negative-flip.
